denial_letter_references.title: ProPublica — Uncovered (insurance denials reporting)
denial_letter_references.relevance: Calibrated P2 letter surface: Circular medical-necessity boilerplate.

denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

UnitedHealthcare has completed its medical necessity review of the prior authorization request submitted by your provider, received at our office on June 8, 2026. This letter constitutes our coverage determination dated June 10, 2026.

After review against MCG Care Guidelines, we are unable to approve the requested service. A lower level of care is appropriate for your condition: routine outpatient therapy is the indicated level of care, and the requested intensive multidisciplinary program exceeds what is required. The available documentation does not establish that outpatient management has been exhausted, and the requested service therefore does not meet the applicable criteria.

This determination was made by a UnitedHealthcare Medical Director, a family-medicine physician, based on the records available for review.

You or your provider may request a copy of the specific criteria applied to this determination by contacting the number on your member ID card. A peer-to-peer discussion may be requested.

If you disagree with this decision, you have the right to file an internal appeal within 180 days of the date of this letter, and you may request an external review by an independent review organization. Appeals may be submitted in writing to UnitedHealthcare Appeals, P.O. Box 740816, Atlanta, GA 30374, or by calling the number on your member ID card.

We understand this decision may be disappointing. This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
UnitedHealthcare Utilization Management
clinical_context: The patient is a 48-year-old woman with persistent post-concussion syndrome (G44.309) five months after a motor-vehicle collision. Her daily headache, vertigo, light sensitivity, and cognitive slowing have kept her out of work and have not improved with routine outpatient care. Over the past three months she completed a course of vestibular physical therapy and saw a neuro-optometrist and a speech-language therapist, but these uncoordinated single-discipline visits did not help. Her neurologist then referred her to an intensive multidisciplinary concussion rehabilitation day program, in which neurology, vestibular therapy, neuropsychology, and occupational therapy are coordinated under one structured plan — the recommended next step once standard outpatient therapy has failed in persistent post-concussion syndrome. The submitted records document the failed outpatient trials, her Rivermead post-concussion symptom questionnaire score, and the degree of functional impairment that keeps her from returning to work.

matrix_coverage.reasoning: matrix_cell matches the assigned cell field-for-field; G44.309 is neurology; multidisciplinary concussion program is realistic; rationale instantiates level_of_care_too_high.

scenario_realism.reasoning: Level-of-care downgrade of an intensive concussion program is a real UHC tactic; vague MCG citation and a family-medicine reviewer on a neurology question are authentic exploitable defects; outpatient already exhausted.

insurer_voice.reasoning: Correct UHC branding and 'documentation does not support' framing, but the step_therapy_vague_mcg flaw forces an MCG citation, which reads slightly Cigna-flavored for a UHC letter.
insurer_voice.improvement: MCG citation is required by the intended flaw; otherwise UHC voice is intact.

denial_logic.reasoning: Circular level-of-care logic citing MCG without thresholds, unfalsifiable 'outpatient not exhausted', criteria withheld; authentically shoddy, fits level_of_care_too_high.

clinical_realism.reasoning: Persistent PCS 5 months post-MVC, failed single-discipline outpatient (vestibular PT, neuro-optometry, SLP), Rivermead questionnaire, multidisciplinary day program is the guideline next step.

diagnosis_treatment_match.reasoning: Multidisciplinary rehab is the evidence-based standard for persistent PCS; clean match.

diversity_delta.reasoning: Only the payer+level_of_care tag overlaps one small-fiber-neuropathy/skin-biopsy case; dx (PCS) and tx (rehab day program) are entirely distinct.

diversity_statistical.reasoning: Max trigram-Jaccard: corpus=0.186 (nearest=case_110_uhc_mednec), neighbours=0.0. Thresholds: warn>=0.35, fail>=0.5.

safety_redactor.reasoning: No banned topics; single-event MVC trauma is acceptable; adult 48F; in-scope commercial plan.

contradiction_hunter.reasoning: Gender, diagnosis, treatment, dates stable; the 'outpatient not exhausted' claim is an appealable falsehood the record rebuts, not an unstable scenario fact.

llm_tell_detector.reasoning: Formal bureaucratic boilerplate and realistic chart prose; no GPT vocabulary, listicles, or meta-commentary; polish/completeness alone are not tells.

overall_tone.reasoning: Cold procedural UHC register with realistic vagueness, no apology; neutral clinical narrative.

financial_auditor.reasoning: Silent on cost liability (standard practice); no dollar figures; no Medicare/Medicaid conflation.

legal_auditor.reasoning: No invented statutes or Medicare framing, but appeal/external-review rights are cleanly granted rather than strategically imperfect; generic legal language.
legal_auditor.improvement: Could omit external review or bury the appeal deadline for a more realistic attack vector.

demographic_validator.reasoning: 48F with persistent post-concussion syndrome after MVC is epidemiologically sound; consistent female pronouns; no stereotype.

scope_guard.reasoning: UHC in-scope, self-funded employer-sponsored commercial, adult 48, no live-filing/representation language.

date_sanity.reasoning: Received Jun 8 / determined Jun 10 2026 in correct order; 180-day window standard; clinical timeline (5 months post-MVC, 3 months of trials) stacks plausibly.

citation_traceability.reasoning: Real MCG brand named at umbrella level with no module/edition/code; criteria withheld pending request; realistically vague.

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Factually false 'outpatient not exhausted' premise (documented failed single-discipline trials), vague MCG citation, and a family-medicine reviewer on a neurology level-of-care question. Easy appeal.
exploitable_weaknesses: Factually false premise: denial says outpatient management not exhausted, but records document failed vestibular PT, neuro-optometry, and speech therapy over three months; the program is the guideline-recommended next step after outpatient failure

Vague MCG citation with no module/edition and criteria withheld, plus reviewer-specialty mismatch (a family-medicine Medical Director adjudicating an intensive neuro-concussion-rehab level-of-care question)
strong_defenses: The denial invokes a recognized step-therapy framework (MCG) requiring exhaustion of lower-level outpatient care before intensive multidisciplinary programs — a legitimate criterion the insurer can argue applies, shifting the dispute to whether documentation sufficiently proves exhaustion
